does: This paper introduces PLOT, a progressive localization framework that identifies which neural sites correspond to causal variables in a neural network for interpretability purposes. Rather than exhaustively searching over candidate neural locations, PLOT leverages optimal transport theory to match the output effects of abstract causal interventions with those of neural interventions across different granularities. The method starts coarsely (tokens, layers, timesteps) and progressively refines to finer-grained locales (neuron groups, PCA subspaces), fitting optimal transport couplings at each level to determine soft correspondences between abstract variables and neural sites that yield interpretable intervention handles.
connects: This work builds on the causal abstraction framework, which grounds mechanistic interpretability by formalizing alignment between high-level causal models and low-level neural computations through counterfactual interventions. It directly extends prior work like Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) that learns subspace interventions but faced computational bottlenecks in site discovery. PLOT connects to the broader optimal transport literature, repurposing transport couplings—a tool used in distribution matching and alignment problems—to solve the neural localization problem. The progressive coarse-to-fine refinement strategy parallels hierarchical search methods in neural network analysis and relates to multi-scale interpretability approaches that operate across different levels of abstraction.
enables: This framework enables more efficient discovery of causal structure in large neural networks, potentially scaling mechanistic interpretability to modern large models where exhaustive search is prohibitive. By replacing brute-force site enumeration with principled transport-based matching, PLOT could unlock faster iteration cycles for interpretability researchers studying model internals. The progressive refinement approach suggests avenues for hierarchical decomposition of neural computations and could inspire similar transport-based methods for other alignment or localization problems in deep learning. More broadly, efficient causal variable localization may support automated discovery of interpretable model components and contribute to safer, more transparent neural network design.
